instruction: Troubleshoot the 'object bbDensity not found' error when running baySeq on a dataset of bacterial community sequences sampled from 6 patients at 4 time points, and advise whether baySeq can handle 4 repeated measures.
input: Error in .nextMethod(.Object, ...) : object 'bbDensity' not found, baySeq, pairCD <- new("countData", data = list(pairData[,1:4], pairData[,5:8]), replicates = c(1,1,2,2), groups = list(NDE = c(1,1,1,1), DE = c(1,1,2,2)), densityFunction = bbDensity
output:
You are using an old version of baySeq. Update to 2.0.50 from Bioconductor and this should work. This will also allow you to use a densityFunction called mdDensity - this is an extension to the beta-binomial analysis of paired data that allows multiple matched samples to be analysed.

Example code; here cdat is a list, each element of which is a matrix corresponding to all the data for a single timepoint. Columns are matched; i.e., cdat[[1]][,1] refers to the same patient as cdat[[2]][,1].

library(baySeq)
cl <- makeCluster(24)
cD <- new("countData", data = cdat[1:10], replicates = c(1,1,1,1,2,2,2,2), groups =\ list(NDE = c(1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1), DE = c(1,1,1,1,2,2,2,2)))
densityFunction(cD) <- mdDensity
libsizes(cD) <- getLibsizes(cD)
cD <- getPriors(cD, cl = cl)
cD <- getLikelihoods(cD, cl = cl)

This works reasonably well, though if the number of timepoints is too great than the empirical prior estimation becomes too sparse. There will be an improved version released to the Bioconductor development thread shortly which overcomes this problem.

instruction: Explain why filtering low-count genes before fitting an edgeR GLM leads to detection of differentially expressed genes, whereas skipping filtering results in none passing significance thresholds.
input: edgeR, GLM, cpm filtering, TMM normalization
output: Filtering low‑count genes reduces the severity of the multiple‑testing correction by removing genes that lack enough evidence to reject the null, thereby decreasing the correction penalty for the remaining p‑values and increasing power. It also removes genes with high dispersion that distort the mean–dispersion trend, which would otherwise overestimate dispersions of high‑abundance genes during empirical Bayes shrinkage. The resulting lower dispersion estimates improve power for detecting differential expression. Additionally, with TMM normalization via calcNormFactors, filtering reduces low‑count genes that produce discrete M‑values, leading to more reliable normalization factors and a more stable downstream analysis.

instruction: Help me understand why DESeq2 does not allow running without replicates and how to proceed with exploratory analysis.
input: DESeq2, Bowtie2, HTSeq, MulticoreParam(12), no replicates error
output: DESeq2 is a tool to test statistical hypotheses, i.e., to calculate p values. Without replicates you cannot obtain p values. For exploratory data analysis, consider using the rlog transformation and then look at genes with highest variance across conditions.

instruction: Assess if the grouped contig counts for KOs and pathways are appropriate for the transformation methods rlog and vts.
output: Can you describe the distribution of the counts across samples more? The transformations are in fact designed for negative binomial-distributed data. Many zeros is not in itself a problem, but if you have, within a row, mostly zeros combined with a minority of very large counts, that's not consistent with a negative binomial distribution. So then I would look for some other transformations.

instruction: Analyze metagenome data with DESeq2 to find genes differentially abundant in different treatments over time, normalizing gene abundance per 16S rRNA copy number which changes data from counts to decimals. Provide a possible way to analyze such data using DESeq2.
output: The way to normalize in DESeq2 if you have a matrix is to not divide the counts, but to provide this matrix to estimateSizeFactor's normMatrix argument. You should divide out the geometric mean of each row of your matrix before providing it to estimateSizeFactors.

instruction: Explain the results of MRfulltable from fitZig in metagenomeSeq, including the meaning of the p-value, coefficient, odds ratio, how variable order affects the output, the handling of continuous variables, and the distinction between count-part and zero-part models.
output:
Question 1. See ?MRfulltable. In the MRfulltable function there are two options: by: Column number or column name specifying which coefficient or contrast of the linear model is of interest. coef: Column number(s) or column name(s) specifying which coefficient or contrast of the linear model to display. By default the second column (by = 2) for displaying p‑values, etc.  

Question 2. The # samples, etc. is only meant for the categorical comparisons.  

Question 3. Same thing – log2 fold change depending on the design.  

Question 4. Odds ratio is from the presence/absence test. It is the same as the results of fitPA (fit presence/absence).  

Question 5. It doesn’t make sense.  

Question 6. See http://www.nature.com/nmeth/journal/v10/n12/full/nmeth.2658.html. fitZig fits a zero‑inflated Gaussian mixture model and estimates weights for the zeros when doing the regression.  

Edit: Additionally, if you have a non‑categorical coefficient you should not be using MRfulltable. That function in particular runs fitPA on your two groups of interest. See ?MRfulltable, ?MRtable, ?MRcoefs for alternative table outputs.

instruction: Explain why the error occurs when running fitTimeSeries(CE_obj_file_aband_graph, lvl='Class', feature='c__Clostridia', id='X.SampleID', time='Age', B=10, norm=F, log=F) and how to resolve it.
output:
The error is caused by a missing parameter. You need to include the case/control status parameter. For example:

library(metagenomeSeq)
setwd("~/Downloads/Data")  # your path
fd <- read.csv("CE_obj_featureData.csv", row.names=1)
pd <- read.csv("CE_obj_phenoData.csv", row.names=1)
counts <- read.csv("mat_counts.tsv", sep="\t", row.names=1)
obj <- newMRexperiment(counts, phenoData=AnnotatedDataFrame(pd), featureData=AnnotatedDataFrame(fd))
obj <- filterData(obj)
fit <- fitTimeSeries(obj, lvl='Class', class='Flock', feature='c__Clostridia', id='X.SampleID', time='Age', B=10, norm=TRUE, log=TRUE)

instruction: Analyze why DESeq2 1.8.1 produces drastically different differential expression results compared to DESeq2 1.4.5 on a microbiome dataset, considering version changes, low counts, and phyloseq/QIIME integration.
input: DESeq2 1.4.5, DESeq2 1.6.3, DESeq2 1.8.1, R3.1, R3.2.1, phyloseq, QIIME
output: The issue is that the dataset has very few rows and low mean normalized counts (most rows <10). This limits robust estimation of global parameters like dispersion prior and LFC prior. Few changes occurred after v1.4, but relevant changes are documented in the NEWS file. It appears that an alternate method for beta prior variance calculation in nbinomWaldTest was added, which helps produce more robust prior variance estimates when many genes have small counts and highly variable MLE log fold changes. Turning off the betaPrior may help.

instruction: Explain why parallelization in curatedMetagenomicData slowed down performance and analyze the provided example.
output:
Here are several examples that illustrate the cost of parallel evaluation:

1. library(BiocParallel) > v = integer(1e8) > system.time(lapply(1:8, function(i, v) i, v)) → user 0.004, system 0.000, elapsed 0.001.

2. Cost of starting up the nodes: system.time(bplapply(1:8, function(i, v) i)) → user 0.148, system 0.012, elapsed 0.481.

3. Cost of transferring data to the workers: system.time(bplapply(1:8, function(i, v) i, v)) → user 0.092, system 0.476, elapsed 1.727.

4. Cost of retrieving data from the workers: system.time(bplapply(1:8, function(i, v) v, v)) → user 0.600, system 1.704, elapsed 3.378.

5. And of course the dominant cost, iteration instead of vectorization: system.time(1:8) → user 0, system 0, elapsed 0.

It seems likely that you've replaced a vectorized calculation with an iteration, and are moving large amounts of data to and from the workers. bpvec() might be a better fit to your needs. And generally, the iteration over n assays implies potentially polynomial scaling, where the first assay is copied in the first iteration, then the first and second assays in the second iteration, then the first, second, and third assays in the third iteration, etc; one would rather develop a more efficient algorithm.

instruction: Use edgeR to perform TOST equivalence tests to compare two groups and identify genes that are significantly similar.
output:
This is not an easy thing to do, as we cannot work with the log-fold changes directly as we would in limma. Rather, we need to test against the upper and lower log-fold change thresholds of the TOST through the offsets. Assume we have a DGEList named y for which we have estimated the dispersions, a design matrix and a coefficient of interest coef for our DE comparison. We could then test against the null hypothesis that the coefficient is set at the upper TOST threshold. In this case, we use a 3‑fold change as the threshold.

orig.offs <- getOffset(y)
y$offset <- orig.offs + log(3)*design[,coef]
fit.up <- glmFit(y, design)
lrt.up <- glmLRT(fit.up, coef=coef)

We do the same for the lower TOST threshold.

y$offset <- orig.offs - log(3)*design[,coef]
fit.down <- glmFit(y, design)
lrt.down <- glmLRT(fit.down, coef=coef)

We then compute the one‑sided p‑values for each direction, and intersect them to get those with log‑fold changes that lie between the upper and lower TOST boundaries. Those that are outside get a p‑value of 1.

p.up <- ifelse(lrt.up$table$logFC < 0, lrt.up$table$PValue/2, 1)
p.down <- ifelse(lrt.down$table$logFC > 0, lrt.down$table$PValue/2, 1)
p.tost <- pmax(p.up, p.down)

So, as you can see, it's not a pleasant process, and it's pretty conservative. You'll probably need to use fairly wide TOST thresholds to get any significance. Obviously, if you're planning to apply this genome‑wide, you'll have to do a BH correction on p.tost.

instruction: Explain whether overlap of edgeR, DESeq2, and limma results gives higher confidence in identified genes, and whether the tools test the same concepts.
output: edgeR and DESeq2 both offer a likelihood ratio test (LRT) based on a negative binomial GLM. DESeq2 also offers a Wald test, and edgeR offers a quasi‑likelihood (QL) F‑test. limma uses ordinary linear model t‑tests and F‑tests. All three use empirical Bayes to shrink dispersion or variance estimates toward the dataset mean. The LRTs of edgeR and DESeq2 are not identical because they use slightly different dispersion estimates; if forced to use the same dispersion, the results would be identical. The Wald test and LRT are asymptotically equivalent, though with few samples the equivalence is less close. DESeq2 shrinks logFC toward zero with the Wald test but not with the LRT; the shrinkage is similar for logFC and standard errors, so it has little effect on p‑values. LRT and Wald tests are more liberal than the QL test and limma because they estimate dispersions separately and then fit a GLM assuming perfect estimation, which can lead to more false positives (e.g., many significant genes on a null comparison). The QL test and limma are more conservative, yielding fewer significant genes but generally maintaining proper false‑positive control. limma’s normal‑distribution approximation breaks down at low counts, and its performance improves with deeper sequencing and more samples. limma is also much faster due to its linear‑model approach and can fit experimental designs with unequal variances or some mixed models that edgeR and DESeq2 cannot handle, though this is likely irrelevant if a design works for all three tools. In practice, DESeq2, edgeR, and limma give broadly similar results; differences are minor compared with the importance of exploratory data analysis to choose the model that best fits the data. It is often useful to run more than one tool for diagnostics, such as viewing BCV/dispersion plots with edgeR or DESeq2 when using limma, or using limma’s voomWithQualityWeights to spot outliers when planning to use edgeR. Generally, for many well‑sequenced samples limma is recommended for speed and performance, while for few samples or low depth DESeq2 or edgeR are preferable. A preference for the QL test is common when strict false‑positive control is desired. The overlap of the three methods does not substantially add evidence beyond what each method already tests; choosing the most appropriate tool for the experiment is more valuable than averaging or overlapping results.

instruction: Explain how read counts fit the negative binomial distribution, including the Bernoulli trials interpretation or alternative equivalent characterization, and justify its use for modeling read counts.
output: In addition to James MacDonald's answer, here's another relevant passage from the same Wikipedia article: "The negative binomial distribution also arises as a continuous mixture of Poisson distributions (i.e. a compound probability distribution) where the mixing distribution of the Poisson rate is a gamma distribution." The other important bit of information to know is that read counts for a sample in theory follow a Binomial(n,p) distribution, where n is the total number of reads and p is the probability of a read mapping to a specific gene. However, the binomial distribution is computationally inconvenient, since it involves computing factorials, and with millions of reads in each sample, the Poisson distribution (with lambda = n*p) is an excellent approximation to the Binomial while being far more mathematically tractable. So the Poisson noise quantifies the counting uncertainty, while the gamma distribution quantifies the variation in gene expression between replicates. The mixture of the two yields the negative binomial. See also section 2 of http://www.statsci.org/smyth/pubs/edgeRChapterPreprint.pdf

instruction: Convert Ensembl gene IDs of differentially expressed genes to Gene Symbols in R, ensuring all IDs are mapped.
input: DESeq2, AnnotationDbi, org.Hs.eg.db, mapIds, EnsDb.Hsapiens.v75, EnsDb.Hsapiens.v79
output: You could use ensembldb to map Ensembl gene IDs to gene names or symbols. Provide an EnsDb package such as EnsDb.Hsapiens.v75 for GRCh37 or EnsDb.Hsapiens.v79 for GRCh38. Use the same AnnotationDbi call but supply the EnsDb object instead of org.Hs.eg.db. Note that the gene names listed by Ensembl are not the same as gene symbols; for protein‑coding genes the names usually match HGNC symbols.

instruction: Determine whether HTSeq + CQN + DESeq2 is equivalent to Salmon --gcBias + tximport + DESeq2, or whether Salmon without --gcBias plus tximport plus CQN plus DESeq2 is needed for correcting GC and transcript length bias for gene expression quantification intended for DESeq2 and eQTL analysis.
input: Salmon, HTSeq, CQN, DESeq2, Tximport, DESeqDataSetFromTximport, --gcBias
output: Salmon’s bias correction (e.g. --gcBias) is performed during quantification and is more direct and comprehensive than a post‑hoc approach such as CQN on gene‑level counts. The bias is estimated jointly with transcript abundances, allowing Salmon to adjust for GC and other biases before the data are passed through tximport to downstream tools. While CQN can still capture much of the remaining gene‑level GC and length bias after Salmon, it is not required for the primary bias correction; Salmon + tximport is the preferred workflow for DESeq2 or eQTL analysis.

instruction: Assist with processing 8 mm9 aligned BAM files using summarizeOverlaps without causing computer freezes.
output: You might try the featureCounts() function in the Rsubread package. Comparisons published in: http://bioinformatics.oxfordjournals.org/content/30/7/923 show that it is considerably faster than compareOverlaps and uses considerably less memory. The efficiency gain is more than an order of magnitude for both time and memory, and is even greater if you have an unusually large number of genomic features. featureCounts() can make use of multiple cores (even without the BiocParallel package).

instruction: Check whether the provided EdgeR code for analyzing RNA‑seq data is correct and reliable, and give feedback on potential issues.
output: There seem to be multiple problems. The filtering step only removes duplicated genes; it should remove low‑abundance genes, otherwise the mean‑dispersion trend will be affected, power will be reduced, and computational time increased. The design matrix is built from the cells factor, implying no replicates; it should be built from status. Mixing classic edgeR (estimateTrendedDisp, estimateCommonDisp) with GLM edgeR (glmFit, glmLRT) is unnecessary; choose one approach. The line y d1$common.dispersion is syntactically invalid. You don't need to reorder by abundance, and you can recalculate library sizes with keep.lib.sizes=FALSE during subsetting. Repeating dispersion estimation calls is also unnecessary.

instruction: Determine if DESeq accounts for different sequencing depth, and identify any additional normalizations or considerations needed for proper analysis.
output: The three resequencing samples are technical replicates and do not constitute a batch effect, and there is no depth effect. Combine all reads as if the sample were run in multiple lanes.

instruction: Use Rsubread's align() function to align paired-end gzFASTQ files, check for Phred offset warnings, determine whether the warnings are misleading, and confirm that mapping results are correct.
input: Rsubread, zcat, awk, haskell script phred.hs, R, warning: The specified phred-score offset (33) seems to be incorrect. The observed phred-score range is [35,84], align(index="genome", readfile1="120521--FC427--L8--3of6--1-02059--RA--SeqInst3--R1.fq.gz", readfile2="120521--FC427--L8--3of6--1-02059--RA--SeqInst3--R2.fq.gz", input_format="gzFASTQ", output_format="BAM", output_file="test.bam", tieBreakHamming=TRUE, unique=TRUE, indels=5, nthreads=1, PE_orientation="fr")
output: The warning message is misleading; it results from a bug in the code that tests the Phred offset. The align() function correctly reads the read sequences and quality scores and uses the user-specified offset of 33. The Phred scores of the data are within the typical range of 0 to 41. Therefore, the mapping results are correct and the warning can be ignored.

instruction: Compare aligned reads back to the reference sequence and enumerate all mismatches and indels per position across all reads.
output: Use Rsamtools::pileup to generate a pileup summary of bases, strands, indels, and read positions per genomic position. The function now supports insertions and returns data that can be processed further. For performance, use a ScanBamParam. Refer to the examples section for guidance.

instruction: Get random samples from a BAM file using scanBam on a BamSampler instance and find a faster way to fetch random entries from a large BAM file.
input: BamSampler, scanBam, ScanBamParam, scanBamWhat, samtools view -s
output: BamSampler reads the whole file, as does samtools view -s, so it is expensive; using yieldSize=10 means R reads only 10 records at a time, but a larger value such as 1,000,000 will fit in memory and speed the operation. scanBamWhat() requests all fields; requesting only the fields needed will be more efficient, e.g. using readGAlignments(). The what=scanBamWhat() approach is only about 10% faster than readGAlignments. Using yieldSize=1,000,000 with readGAlignments() on a ~2 Gb file takes ~70 s, about twice as long as samtools view -s, and does not create an intermediate BAM file. There is no other known way to draw a random sample directly; biocvizBase uses an index‑based trick in estimateCoverage,BamFile-method. For a general solution, GenomicFiles::reduceByYield() can iterate over a BAM file with an arbitrary function to yield data and a reduce function to sample. For example, define a reduce function REDUCEsampler that keeps a sample of a given size and use reduceByYield(bf, yield, identity, REDUCEsampler(sampleSize, verbose)).

Then read the relevant reads with scanBam(bfl, param=ScanBamParam(which=which)). The error comes from constructing a GRanges incorrectly; instead use which <- GRanges("ref2", IRanges(1, 162114)).

Use Rsamtools , specifying a ScanBamParam() with just the information you'd like to extract. Coerce the result to a data.frame (it's just a list anyway, so this is inexpensive). 
> library(Rsamtools) 
> fl = system.file(package="Rsamtools", "extdata", "ex1.bam") 
> p = ScanBamParam(what=c("rname", "pos")) 
> head(as.data.frame(scanBam(fl, param=p))) 
rname pos
1 seq1 1
2 seq1 3
3 seq1 5
4 seq1 6
5 seq1 9
6 seq1 13
The help pages ?ScanBamParam and ?scanBam and the package vignette browseVignettes("Rsamtools") as well as the package GenomicAlignments are likely to be helpful.

instruction: Describe ways to optimize the Rsamtools process of summarizing read lengths when filtering using genomic coordinates to reduce slowness and memory usage.
output: The slowness and memory use is because each range is a separate call – 2442918 calls instead of one, creating 2442918 DNAStringSets instead of one. If many ranges, use one call and subsetByOverlaps, or one call per chromosome. Beware that one-range-at-a-time counts reads overlapping two queries twice. Use qwidth instead of seq for read length. Less low-level work can be achieved using GenomicAlignments::readGAlignments instead of scanBam.

instruction: Determine whether to use raw counts or normalized log2 counts from voom for calculating percentage distributions of reads among transcript categories for stacked bar plots.
output: Use the original counts, e.g., those from featureCounts, to calculate percentage distributions between transcript types. Normalized log-counts from voom are not suitable for this application, as summing log-counts would effectively multiply the original counts. Voom automatically incorporates normalization factors when the input is a DGEList; you can create a DGEList from your count data, run calcNormFactors (or supply your own normalization factors), and then supply the resulting object to voom.

instruction: How should I perform windowCounts with csaw for MeDIP data when different bam file lists produce differing numbers of windows, and should I generate window counts for all samples together or separately per group?
input: csaw, windowCounts, readParam(max.frag=650, pe="first",dedup=FALSE), R, RangedSummarizedExperiment, SummarizedExperiment
output: If you're going to do differential analyses on all of them together, why not count them all together? This would be the simplest and easiest method by far. You'll end up with a count for each sample in each window, which is what you'll need to perform DB comparisons between samples anyway. If you really want to count them separately, you can set filter=0 in windowCounts . This turns off the filtering that causes you to get different numbers of windows between windowCounts calls, such that you can cbind the objects that you get out. However, this will return counts for every window in the genome, which will chew through your RAM quite quickly. Separate counting for each group is not recommended. This is for practical reasons, as it's hard to match up windows between RangedSummarizedExperiment objects, especially for DB sites where the window might be missing in a group; and statistical reasons, as you'll end up enriching for windows where the null hypothesis is more likely to be rejected (possibly spuriously), which biases the downstream DB analysis between groups.

instruction: Use DESeq2 to normalize Salmon output count matrix imported with tximport. Also, explain if a similar normalization can be performed on the abundance output of Salmon.
output: If you mean scaling counts for sequencing depth, create a DESeqDataSet with DESeqDataSetFromTximport(txi, ~condition) and then get normalized counts with counts(dds, normalized=TRUE). DESeq2 does not operate on abundance data; it uses counts alone. If you want to scale TPM using a median-ratio procedure, you can manually calculate TPM size factors with estimateSizeFactorsForMatrix.

instruction: When running readVCF on 1000 genomes data from 20130502, no genotype is returned, likely because readVCF expects a FORMAT header for GT. Provide a clear instruction on how to resolve this issue.
output: This is a known issue. ReadVCF has a TODO to parse the standard fields listed in VCF 1.4.2 even when they do not have a header line. As a workaround, add a header line for GT to your file. Variants without GT are read in as missing ('.|.' etc.).

instruction: Explain why altSnp[altSnp] is used in the isSnp function and whether the function returns TRUE only for biallelic SNPs with alternate allele A, C, G, or T, and whether checking the reference allele is necessary.
output: altSnp[altSnp] is used because ai aligns with a[altSnp] and a aligns with altSnp. The code only considers biallelic SNPs. It could be rewritten using the atomic list API: refSnp <- nchar(ref(x)) == 1L; altSnp <- any(nchar(alt(x)) == 1L & alt(x) %in% DNA_LETTERS); refSnp & altSnp. It would be simpler to call the built‑in isSNV function. For CollapsedVCF it also only considers biallelic SNPs by default, but setting singleAltOnly=FALSE gives the same result. It may return a LogicalList for CollapsedVCF because alt is a list, and it might not work for alt values of "*". Checking ref(x) for DNA letters is unnecessary because it is a DNAStringSet.

instruction: Convert possibly-uncertain genotypes from a VCF to a snpMatrix object and determine if PL support is available.
output: VariantAnnotation 1.17.8 adds support for the PL field. It should be available later today after propagating through the build process.

instruction: Write a unitary test with testthat that compares ref(vcf) correctly, avoiding differences in internal representation.
input: testthat, ref(vcf), DNAStringSet, VCF, GRanges, IRanges, VariantAnnotation::filterVcf, expect_equal, as.character
output: Hi, expect_equal(as.character(ref(observed)), as.character(ref(expected))) passes and that's all what matters. If you worry about performance, you could replace it with testing that all(ref(observed) == ref(expected)) is TRUE, which is going to be much more efficient because it avoids the costly coercion from DNAStringSet to character. However, ref(observed) == ref(expected) doesn't compare the names or the metadata columns, only the DNA sequences in the 2 objects, so these are things you would need to check explicitly if they matter to you. Now the fact that expect_equal(ref(observed), ref(expected)) fails is irrelevant because there is more than 1 possible internal representation for the same DNAStringSet object. For example: dna1 <- DNAStringSet(c("AC", "AC")) dna2 <- as(matchPattern("AC", subseq(DNAString("TTCACAC"), start=2)), "DNAStringSet") dna1 and dna2 represent the same thing (i.e. they are indistinguishable from a user point of view): dna1 # A DNAStringSet instance of length 2 # width seq # [1] 2 AC # [2] 2 AC dna2 # A DNAStringSet instance of length 2 # width seq # [1] 2 AC # [2] 2 AC However their internal representations differ: dna1@ranges@start # [1] 1 3 dna2@ranges@start # [1] 4 6 The bottom line is that it's not a good idea to compare equality of internal representations in your tests. It's better to check that your results look the expected way by inspecting them thru the user interface. More generally speaking, and as Martin said, the internal representation of S4 objects is "none of the end-user business", and the less you know about it the better. Hope this makes sense, Cheers, H.

instruction: Import Platypus VCF files into R using VariantAnnotation VRanges despite END field specification differences.

instruction: Keep only the pass entries in the filter column of a large VCF file using Variant Annotation.

instruction: Determine which version of the GO database is used in ClusterProfiler and explain why the background size numbers differ from Panther webtool.
input: clusterProfiler, Panther webtool, useMart, getBM, enrichGO
output:
ClusterProfiler uses the GO information available in the GO.db package. The GO annotation database is updated twice a year before each new Bioconductor release. For the latest Bioconductor release (3.5) the GO data was collected on 29 March 2017 (GO.db version 3.4.1). For example:

output: Simply download a GTF file (for example from GENCODE) matching the current genome and/or annotation version you are using in your project and get it from there. instruction: Add additional information (such as gene symbols, chromosomal coordinates, etc.) from a data frame to a DESeqDataSet that was created from a count table with Ensembl gene IDs as row names.
output: DESeqDataSet is a subclass of SummarizedExperiment, and you can add to the metadata columns of the rowData with code like mcols(dds) <- cbind(mcols(dds), newMcols). Make sure the new columns line up row‑for‑row with the DESeqDataSet: all(rownames(dds) == annotation$ensembl_gene_id). If they do not match, reorder the annotation data frame using match() so that Ensembl IDs align, then check again: annotation <- annotation[match(rownames(dds), annotation$ensembl_gene_id),] all(rownames(dds) == annotation$ensembl_gene_id). The heatmap.2 function uses the rownames of the matrix you give it, so you could assign the mgi_symbols as the rownames of the DESeqDataSet, but only if each mgi_symbol is unique. Check this with sum(is.na(annotation$mgi_symbol)) and sum(duplicated(annotation$mgi_symbol)). If both sums are zero, then rownames(dds) <- mcols(dds)$mgi_symbol will work; otherwise supply the labels via the labRow argument of heatmap.2. To include the mgi symbol in the results table, use: res <- results(dds); res$mgi_symbol <- mcols(dds)$mgi_symbol. The ReportingTools vignette may provide details on how to specify which columns appear in the final report.

instruction: Determine the most appropriate method for handling probeids that map ambiguously to several genes when annotating HTA 2.0 chip data, and explain the biological reason behind these unambiguous mappings.
input: RMA, AnnotationDbi::select, hta20sttranscriptcluster.db, SYMBOL, GENENAME, ENTREZID, ENSEMBL
output: You can annotate your ExpressionSet using annotateEset in the affycoretools package, which explicitly allows you to control for the multiple mappings. There are levels of multiple mappings: different annotation groups (NCBI, EBI/EMBL) and within groups (HUGO symbols versus aliases) can disagree, and Entrez Gene IDs can change over time. Probe sets may also span overlapping genes, introns, or miRNAs, leading to many‑to‑one or one‑to‑many mappings. Typical approaches include taking the first annotation of a multi‑mapping probe (the naive approach), selecting the probe set with the largest fold change using findLargest, using an MBNI re‑mapped probe set, or reporting the top set of probe sets and inspecting inconsistencies. The choice depends on the intended audience and analysis goals.

instruction: Create an R script that parses two BED files containing genomic regions and associated attributes, merges overlapping regions, and outputs a new file that contains each overlapping region with its corresponding attributes from the original files.
output: Use the rtracklayer and GenomicRanges packages. Load them with library(rtracklayer) and library(GenomicRanges). Import the BED files with import(), which creates GRanges objects. The coordinates are converted from BED’s 0-based, half-open representation to Bioconductor’s 1-based, closed-interval representation. The GRanges objects contain genomic coordinates and metadata columns (mcols). Assume the attribute is named ‘score’. To find the shared ranges, use disjoin() on the ranges of both objects to create a set of non-overlapping ranges. Subset these disjoint ranges to only those that overlap both original objects with dj <- dj[(dj %over% gr1) & (dj %over% gr2)]. Add metadata columns to the disjoint ranges: mcols(dj) <- DataFrame(mappability=NA, NRL=NA). For each attribute, find overlaps between the disjoint ranges and the original GRanges and update the metadata: define a helper function annotate that uses findOverlaps(dj, gr) and assigns the score to the appropriate column. Run annotate(dj, gr1, "mappability") and annotate(dj, gr2, "NRL"). The result is a GRanges object with the overlapping ranges and their attributes. Save the result to a CSV with write.csv(as.data.frame(dj), "my.csv").

instruction: Identify gene symbols and GENE IDs for Clostridium beijerinckii NCIMB 8052 to use in KEGG Expression pathway analysis of RNA‑seq data.
output: Use AnnotationHub to retrieve the Clostridium beijerinckii orgdb. 1. Load the package: library(AnnotationHub). 2. Create a hub object: hub <- AnnotationHub(). 3. Query the hub for records containing "clostridium" and "orgdb": query(hub, c("clostridium","orgdb")). 4. Find the record with the title "org.Clostridium_beijerinckii.eg.sqlite" (record ID AH73787). 5. Access this record: orgdb <- hub[["AH73787"]]. 6. Use the select() function to extract the SYMBOL and ENTREZID columns for the gene keys: select(orgdb, head(keys(orgdb)), c("SYMBOL","ENTREZID")). 7. The ENTREZID corresponds to the NCBI Gene ID, which is the same as the EntrezGene ID. 8. For further details, read the AnnotationDbi vignette, the AnnotationHub vignette, and the AnnotationHub HOWTO.

instruction: Determine which annotation package to use for Affymetrix GeneChip PrimeView and how to annotate from a CSV.
output:
The PrimeView array does not have an official Bioconductor annotation package. You can download the NetAffx annotation CSV from the Affymetrix website and create your own package:

1. Build the database and package:
   r
   require(AnnotationForge)
   makeDBPackage(
     'HUMANCHIP_DB',
     affy = TRUE,
     prefix = 'primeview',
     fileName = 'PrimeView.na36.annot.csv',
     baseMapType = 'eg',
     outputDir = '.',
     author = 'Bioconductor',
     version = '0.99.1',
     manufacturer = 'Affymetrix',
     manufacturerUrl = 'http://www.affymetrix.com'
   )
   

2. Install and load the package:
   r
   install.packages('primeview.db', repos = NULL, type = 'source')
   require(primeview.db)
   

3. Perform lookups, e.g., mapping probe IDs to Ensembl IDs or symbols:
   r
   probes <- c('11715262_at', '1715107_s_at', '11715112_at', '11715113_x_at', '11715115_s_at', '11715116_s_at', '11715247_s_at', '11715248_s_at', '11715258_s_at', '11715269_x_at', '11715264_s_at')
   mapIds(primeview.db, keys = probes, column = c('ENSEMBL'), keytype = 'PROBEID')
   mapIds(primeview.db, keys = probes, column = c('SYMBOL'), keytype = 'PROBEID')
   select(primeview.db, keys = probes, columns = c('PROBEID', 'SYMBOL', 'GENENAME', 'ENSEMBL', 'GO'))
   annotate::getSYMBOL(probes, 'primeview.db')
   

These steps create a usable annotation package for the PrimeView array and allow the use of getSYMBOL and other AnnotationDbi functions.

instruction: Extract three types of intergenic regions (Tendem, convergent, divergent) in a GRanges object from genomic and GFF file.
output:
makeTxDbFromGFF() from GenomicFeatures fails to produce a TxDb. Something in your GFF is not meeting the expected format. This is usually fixable by debugging the GFF. However, you might use Biomart as source of your annotations (GFF) instead. If this is fine then try the following which works just fine:

library(GenomicFeatures)
library("biomaRt")
library(systemPipeR)
txdb <- makeTxDbFromBiomart(biomart = "ensembl", dataset = "scerevisiae_gene_ensembl")
myfeatures <- c("tx_type", "promoter", "intron", "exon", "cds", "intergenic")
feat <- genFeatures(txdb, featuretype=myfeatures, reduce_ranges=FALSE, upstream=1000, downstream=0)

Created feature ranges: protein_coding, ncRNA, tRNA, snoRNA, pseudogene, snRNA, rRNA
Created feature ranges: promoter
Created feature ranges: intron
Created feature ranges: exon
Created feature ranges: cds
Created feature ranges: intergenic

Now the intergenic ranges can be extracted with feat$intergenic. Note: using feature="all" will give you an error for fiveUTR/threeUTR since those return empty objects. I will change this to a warning in the next update of systemPipeR.

Using the latest OrgDb will affect the gene number of annotated GO terms and the total number of annotated GO terms. You can try liftover the peaks to mm10 and re‑annotate the peaks before enrichment analysis. Example code:

> library(ChIPpeakAnno)

> ch <- import.chain("hg19ToHg38.over.chain")

> peaks.hg19 <- annotatePeakInBatch(peaks, AnnotationData=hg19)
> peaks.hg38 <- unlist(peaks.hg38)
> peaks.hg38 <- annotatePeakInBatch(peaks.hg38, AnnotationData=hg38)
> library(org.Hs.eg.db)
> over.hg19 <- getEnrichedGO(peaks.hg19, "org.Hs.eg.db", condense=TRUE)
> over.hg38 <- getEnrichedGO(peaks.hg38, "org.Hs.eg.db", condense=TRUE)
> dim(over.hg19$bp) [1] 210 10
> dim(over.hg38$bp) [1] 199 10
> setdiff(over.hg38$bp[1:20, 1], over.hg19$bp[1:20, 1]) [1] "GO:0001503" "GO:0001657"

instruction: Find probe IDs for a list of RefSeq IDs using Bioconductor mapping functions.
input: annotate, mouse4302.db, revmap, mouse4302REFSEQ, select, mapIds
output: You are using the BiMap interface, which is old now, and has been superceded. One of the problems (IMO) with the BiMap interface is that by default any one-to-many mappings are ignored, and you just get an NA returned. You could get around this by using toggleProbes(), but not many people know about that. The 'modern' way to get annotation data is to use select() or mapIds(). The former method will return all of the one-to-many mappings, and can be used to return multiple annotations at once (e.g., you could get the RefSeq, Entrez Gene, HUGO Symbol, etc all at once). The latter gives you more control over the return object, including how to deal with one-to-many mappings, but only works with one annotation at a time. You have seen what select() returns. Here are the choices for mapIds(): mapIds(mouse4302.db, "1416184_s_at", "REFSEQ","PROBEID") 1416184_s_at "NM_001025427" mapIds(mouse4302.db, "1416184_s_at", "REFSEQ","PROBEID", multiVals = "list") $`1416184_s_at` [1] "NM_001025427" "NM_001039356" "NM_001166535" "NM_001166536" "NM_001166537" [6] "NM_001166539" "NM_001166540" "NM_001166541" "NM_001166542" "NM_001166543" [11] "NM_001166544" "NM_001166545" "NM_001166546" "NM_016660" "NP_001020598" [16] "NP_001034445" "NP_001160007" "NP_001160008" "NP_001160009" "NP_001160011" [21] "NP_001160012" "NP_001160013" "NP_001160014" "NP_001160015" "NP_001160016" [26] "NP_001160017" "NP_001160018" "NP_057869" "NM_001166476" "NM_001166477" [31] "NP_001159948" "NP_001159949" mapIds(mouse4302.db, "1416184_s_at", "REFSEQ","PROBEID", multiVals = "filter") NULL mapIds(mouse4302.db, "1416184_s_at", "REFSEQ","PROBEID", multiVals = "asNA") 1416184_s_at NA mapIds(mouse4302.db, "1416184_s_at", "REFSEQ","PROBEID", multiVals = "CharacterList") CharacterList of length 1 [["1416184_s_at"]] NM_001025427 NM_001039356 ... NP_001159948 NP_001159949 Each of the return types has its uses, depending on how you plan to deal with the multi-mapped data.

instruction: Explain whether differences in Over Representation Analysis results when using different reference genome assemblies and AnnotationHub annotation files are normal, and how to control for them.
input: QuantSeq, Atlantic salmon, Icsasg_v2, Ssal_v3.1, clusterProfiler, AnnotationHub, AH107424, AH114250
output:
Run this code on current release: 


library(AnnotationHub)
hub <- AnnotationHub()
z <- hub[["AH114250"]]
zz <- mapIds(z, keys(z), "GOALL", "ENTREZID", multiVals = "list")


Then did the same on an R‑4.2.2 install, using AH107424, and saved as ‘salmo_old.Rds’. Read those results in:


oldzz <- readRDS("salmo_old.Rds")


Compare names:


all.equal(names(zz), names(oldzz))
# [1] "Lengths (65492, 71043) differ (string compare on first 65492)"
# [2] "16390 string mismatches"


Length of sets:


length(zz)   # 65492
length(oldzz)   # 71043


For simplicity use the intersection, but note that the new OrgDb has ~5500 fewer genes:


int <- intersect(names(zz), names(oldzz))
zzz <- mapply(function(x,y) length(setdiff(x,y)), zz[int], oldzz[int])


The table of differences shows most genes differ by one or fewer GO terms. The biggest difference is:


zzz == 119L


For gene 106601220 the new file lists 119 GO terms, while the old file lists only 9 core cellular component terms.

So small differences are expected, but very large differences may require deeper investigation.

instruction: Explain whether users should assume AnnotationHub/ExperimentHub resources are perfectly reproducible in perpetuity and whether users should have a personal backup of important resources.
input: AnnotationHub, ExperimentHub, Bioconductor, Zenodo, Figshare, NIH
output: When you first download a resource from AnnotationHub or ExperimentHub, it is saved in a cache directory on your local machine. Subsequent requests for that resource use the cached copy instead of downloading it again. There is no guarantee that resources will remain perfectly reproducible forever. Bioconductor exists thanks to NIH grants, and many resources depend on those grants. If Bioconductor were targeted, it would be affected like other grant‑dependent projects.

instruction: Determine if the wgEncodeOpenChromDnaseGm12878Pk.narrowPeak.gz resource is broken in AnnotationHub and whether it should be reported here.
output: The issue was with parsing the first line to determine the column count. The import,BEDFile-method parsed based on tabs or spaces, but not a combination of the two. The AH25517 record has a field with tabs followed by spaces. The first line contains extra spaces before the '11': "chr1\t713841\t714424\tchr1.1\t1000\t.\t0.1783\t 11\t-1\t259". Using strsplit() the line was parsed into 12 elements instead of 10: "chr1", "713841", "714424", "chr1.1", "1000", ".", "0.1783", "", "", "11", "-1", "259". This misalignment of vector length with column names and data types caused the "expected an integer" error. A fix has been checked in rtracklayer 1.31.8; if it succeeds on the builds tomorrow it will be ported to release.

instruction: Provide instructions on how to determine if the GSE62944 ExpressionSet includes normal samples, how to subset breast cancer and normal samples from the dataset, and how to subset specific genes from the ExpressionSet.
output:
The ExpressionSet returned for GSE62944 contains only the 7706 tumor samples and does not include any normal samples. 

To subset by cancer type (e.g., breast cancer) use the phenoData slot and the which function:

# subset breast cancer samples
brca_data <- tcga_data[, which(phenoData(tcga_data)$CancerType == "BRCA")]

This approach can be applied to any cancer type.

To subset specific genes from the expression matrix, extract the assay data with `exprs()` and then subset rows using row names:

raw_data <- exprs(brca_data)          # matrix of gene expression
my_genes <- c("PTEN", "MYC", "BRCA1")
idx <- match(tolower(my_genes), tolower(rownames(raw_data)))
selected_data <- raw_data[idx, ]

These steps provide the requested subsetting of samples and genes.

instruction: Explain why the qValue column shows larger -log10 values than the pValue column in the Roadmap annotation files.
output: Hi, Thanks for reporting the bug. These files were stamped with a dispatch class that wasn't distinguishing between narrow and broad peak files which have different metadata columns. The narrow peak files have an extra 'peak' column as shown in the ucsc link you reference. Because of this the metadata columns of the broad peak files were shifted to the right - what was really 'qvalue' was called 'peak', what was really 'pvalue' was called 'qvalue'. A fix is checked into AnnotationHub 2.3.20 and should be available Saturday after noon PST. Let me know if you have other problems. Valerie

instruction: Verify that the RPKM data.frame from AnnotationHub has the gene_id column used as row names and the first numeric column as the expression index for sample E000.
output: Hi Can, Thanks for reporting this bug. As you described, the problem was how the data were read in, the gene_id column was not being used as the row names. This has been fixed in AnnotationHub 2.5.13 (devel) and 2.4.3 (release). Both should be available via biocLite() Thursday Oct. 13 after noon PST or from svn immediately. Valerie

instruction: Identify and correct duplicate or incorrect GRanges resources in AnnotationHub that come from the same ENCODE URL, and determine whether to delete or combine them.
output: The first resource is part of a problematic group of encodeDCC files added to AnnotationHub for hg19. Some files were saved with the wrong file extension, causing them to be unreadable. Metadata were marked as 1-based when they should be 0-based, and some files contain both ‘chrM’ and ‘chrMT’, with ‘chrMT’ appearing to be an error that duplicates ‘chrM’. Fixing these data and metadata would be a large task and likely introduce further errors, so these resources are being removed from the hub so they no longer appear when AnnotationHub() is called. New resources from the same URLs have been added, and the quality control process is now more stringent, with recipes that create them checked regularly. After the next AnnotationHub() call (snapshot Dec 8, 2016), the problematic resource will no longer be visible, and only properly prepared and documented resources will appear.

output:
Use AnnotationHub to query EnsDb for Macaca mulatta:

instruction: Retrieve gene annotations for DiffBind GRanges while preserving the statistical metadata columns.
input: DiffBind, GenomicFeatures::cdsByOverlaps, TxDb.Scerevisiae.UCSC.sacCer3.sgdGene, Salt.DB, findOverlaps, transcriptsBy, org.Sc.sgd.db
output:
The cdsByOverlaps function is intended to get you a set of cds that overlap your ranges. So the new GRanges object won't have any of the statistics because the only way your differentially bound ranges come into play is to see if a given cds region overlaps it.

If instead you want to say what genes a given range overlaps, where you could define 'overlap' in many different ways, depending on how close the binding region has to be to the gene, or if you just care about promoter sites or whatever, then you should use findOverlaps instead, which will give you a Hits object that provides an index for each of your GRanges objects.

So as an example, using completely untested code because I don't have your data, you would do something like


## I would use transcripts rather than cds, but you choose
> txs <- transcriptsBy(TxDb.Scerevisiae.UCSC.sacCer3.sgdGene)
## here I call your GRanges from diffbind 'diffbindGR'
> fo <- findOverlaps(txs, diffbindGR)
## add column to diffbindGR
> mcols(diffbindGR)$gene_id <- NA
## and populate with gene ids
> mcols(diffbindGR)$gene_id[subjectHits(fo)] <- names(txs)[queryHits(fo)]


You could also use the names of the txs object to get the gene symbols if that's better for you, doing something like


> library(org.Sc.sgd.db)
> gns <- mapIds(org.Sc.sgd.db, names(txs), "GENENAME", "ORF")
'select()' returned 1:1 mapping between keys and columns
> head(gns)
Q0010 Q0032 Q0055 Q0075 Q0080 Q0085 NA NA "AI2" "AI5_BETA" "ATP8" "ATP6"
> mcols(diffbindGR)$genename <- NA
> mcols(diffbindGR)$genename[subjectHits(fo)] <- gns[queryHits(fo)]

instruction: Extract the coordinates of intergenic regions on chromosome 1 using transcript information from TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene, and explain what the TxDb object contains and how grouping by gene, exon, or intron differs.
input: IRanges, GenomicFeatures, TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene, transcriptsBy, seqlevels, reduce, findOverlaps
output:
Load the transcript database:

library(TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene)

Get exons for each gene:

exbygene <- exonsBy(TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene, "gene")

The exbygene object is a GRangesList with one entry per gene (~23,500). To get intergenic regions, do:

intergenicRegions <- gaps(unlist(range(exbygene)))

This uses range() to get the start and end of each gene, unlists the GRangesList into a single GRanges, and gaps() returns the intervals between those ranges. To subset chromosome 1:

intergenic.chr1 <- intergenicRegions[seqnames(intergenicRegions) == "chr1",]

instruction: Obtain Saccharomyces cerevisiae sacCer2 genomic features using makeTxDbFromBiomart
output:
This takes some fiddling around in order to get what you want. Since you need an archive version of a species, you first go to www.ensembl.org, then under the 'Browse a Genome' box on the upper left, choose S. cerevisiae. When the new page loads, click on the 'view in archive site' link at the bottom right. That will bring up a box listing all the available archives, the last one for EF4 was Ensembl74 from December 2013. Click that link. The URI for the page that loads is http://dec2013.archive.ensembl.org/Saccharomyces_cerevisiae/Info/Index . We want the first part of that, dec2013.archive.ensembl.org. Now we need to figure out the correct mart name, so do this:
> listMarts(host = "dec2013.archive.ensembl.org")
biomart version 1
ENSEMBL_MART_ENSEMBL
Ensembl Genes 74
2 ENSEMBL_MART_SNP
Ensembl Variation 74
3 ENSEMBL_MART_FUNCGEN
Ensembl Regulation 74
4 ENSEMBL_MART_VEGA
Vega 54
5 pride
PRIDE (EBI UK)
So we want "ENSEMBL_MART_ENSEMBL".
Now note that you can specify things to makeTxDbFromBiomart(), like the mart or host:
> args(makeTxDbFromBiomart)
function (biomart = "ensembl", dataset = "hsapiens_gene_ensembl", transcript_ids = NULL, circ_seqs = DEFAULT_CIRC_SEQS, filters = "", id_prefix = "ensembl_", host = "www.biomart.org", port = 80, taxonomyId = NA, miRBaseBuild = NA)
So we now plug in the information we have.
> z <- makeTxDbFromBiomart("ENSEMBL_MART_ENSEMBL", "scerevisiae_gene_ensembl", host = "dec2013.archive.ensembl.org")

instruction: Write the nucleotide sequences for all transcripts of each gene to a FASTA file, grouped by gene ID.
output: Form a GRangesList with exons grouped by transcript and extract those sequences. Then concatenate the sequences of transcripts from the same gene, ideally after sorting transcripts by gene. For example: load the Homo.sapiens library, create tx <- exonsBy(Homo.sapiens, columns='GENEID', outerMcols=TRUE); adjust gene IDs, filter out NAs, order by gene ID; extract the transcripts with txseqs <- extractTranscriptSeqs(Hsapiens, tx); aggregate by gene: gene.widths <- sum(splitAsList(sum(width(tx)), mcols(tx)$GENEID)); relist(unlist(txseqs), PartitioningByWidth(gene.widths)).

instruction: Explain why restricting seqlevels when retrieving genes from a TxDb may change the number of genes returned, and describe how to adjust the genes() call to avoid this issue.
output: Genes may be ambiguous between main chromosomes and unplaced contigs. When all contigs are present, genes() may drop ambiguous genes; restricting seqlevels resolves the ambiguity, which explains the large difference on chromosome 6. Setting single.strand.genes.only = FALSE in the genes() call changes this behavior. For example, gene_id 100423008 is assigned to chr21 when only chr21 is in seqlevels, to chrUn_gl000213 when only that contig is in seqlevels, and disappears when both are present. Using single.strand.genes.only = FALSE returns both assignments.

instruction: Modify GenomicFeatures makeTxDbPackage to allow specifying provider and providerVersion separately and set SOURCEURL from 'Resource URL', and add arguments to makeTxDbFromGFF to specify provider meta-data.
input: GenomicFeatures, Bioc 3.2, Bioc 3.3, makeTxDbPackage, makeTxDbFromGFF, UCSC, BioMart, Data source, Resource URL
output: PROVIDER is set from the 'Data source' metadata, and PROVIDERVERSION uses 'Data source' only if PROVIDER is neither 'UCSC' nor 'BioMart'. To specify these fields, pass them to makeTxDbPackage() rather than makeTxDbFromGFF(), because 'provider' and 'provider_version' belong to the DESCRIPTION, not the TxDb metadata. The 'provider' and 'providerVersion' arguments were added to makeTxDbPackage(). SOURCEURL is now sourced from 'Resource Url'. These changes are in the devel branch, GenomicFeatures 1.25.3, after the 3.3 release, and are limited to bug fixes.

instruction: Determine a method to return only the first overlapping gene when multiple genes overlap, using genomicfeatures or preprocessing the GTF file before calling makeTxDbFromGFF.
output: makeTxDbFromGFF() imports genes, transcripts, exons, and CDS from a GFF file into a TxDb object. After creating the TxDb, extract the gene coordinates with genes(txdb). Then use findOverlaps(reads, gn, select="first") to retrieve the first overlapping gene for each range.

instruction: Identify why makeTxDbFromGFF fails after updating R and Bioconductor and explain the cause of the GFF3 import error.
input: makeTxDbFromGFF, rtracklayer, GenomicFeatures, GenomicRanges, subscript contains NAs or out-of-bounds indices
output: Your GFF3 file has spaces between the tag=value pairs and the preceding semicolon. The new parser in rtracklayer considered that space part of the tag, so tags became ' Name' and ' Parent', causing problems. This was fixed in rtracklayer 1.32.1 (release) and 1.33.5 (development). Both versions should become available via biocLite in the next 48 hours.

instruction: Identify genomic features (e.g., 5' UTR, exon, gene body, 3' UTR, intergenic) for a list of differentially methylated regions given in chr start end format, using R functions without converting TxDb to GFF.
output: Put your list of regions in a GRanges or GRangesList if not already. No need to convert TxDb to GFF. Use GenomicFeatures functions to extract regions of interest, then call findOverlaps(). See ?transcripts and ?transcriptsBy for functions that extract regions from a TxDb. Alternatively use locateVariants() from VariantAnnotation. It performs findOverlaps between your ranges and a TxDb based on the 'regions' argument. Example: gr is a GRanges of your positions. library(TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene) txdb <- TxDb.Hsapiens.UCSC.hg19.knownGene. Identify coding regions: loc_all <- locateVariants(gr, txdb, CodingVariants()). Identify 5 UTR regions: loc_all <- locateVariants(gr, txdb, FiveUTRVariants()). See ?locateVariants for more region options.

instruction: Extract multimapped RNA‑seq reads from a BAM file aligned with STAR to hg38 and identify overlapping genes using Rsamtools, GenomicAlignments, and GenomicFeatures.
input: BAM file, STAR, hg38, Rsamtools, GenomicAlignments, GenomicFeatures
output:
library(GenomicAlignments)
ambiguousReads <- readGAlignmentPairs("/users/robert/project/example.bam", param = ScanBamParam(flag = scanBamFlag(isSecondaryAlignment = TRUE)))
# Only import multi-mapping reads.

library(rtracklayer)
genesExonsTranscripts <- import("/users/robert/databases/hg38/GENCODEversion26.gtf")
# GRanges with metadata.

genes <- subset(genesExonsTranscripts, type == "gene")

library(GenomicRanges)
genesAlignments <- countOverlaps(genes, ambiguousReads)
mcols(GENCODEgenes)[genesAlignments > 0, "gene_name"]
# Gene symbols of genes with non-zero counts.

# You may like to assign such alignments to a particular gene using RSEM.

instruction: Explain why an error occurs when loading tidyverse before VariantAnnotation and how to fix it so locateVariants works when tidyverse is loaded first.
input: locateVariants, VariantAnnotation, tidyverse, Error in as.vector(x) : no method for coercing this S4 class to a vector
output: After investigation it was found to be an S4 problem. Both lubridate (which tidyverse depends on) and S4Vectors define a "union" S4 generic. Loading both packages creates two "union" generics in the cache, which confuses functions in the methods package such as getGeneric(), standardGeneric() and callNextMethod(). A workaround was added to S4Vectors that resolves the issue. The workaround is available in S4Vectors 0.14.4 (release) and 0.15.7 (devel) and will be available via biocLite() soon.

instruction: Fix the error when using makeTxDbFromBiomart to download data from fungi_mart.
input: makeTxDbFromBiomart, fungi_mart, spombe_eg_gene, host=fungi.ensembl.org, Server denied you to change to the given directory
output: The internal function .get_EnsemblGenomes_kingdom_from_biomart in GenomicFeatures still looks for "fungal_mart". It therefore cannot identify the kingdom and fails to set the host. Edit the source package: download GenomicFeatures, unzip, change the line in R/makeTxDbFromBiomart.R so that it checks for "fungi_mart" (e.g., if (substr(biomart, 1, 10) == "fungi_mart")). Re‑zip the package and install it with R CMD INSTALL. After installing the modified package the TxDb download from Biomart succeeds.

instruction: Fix the issue where makeTxDbFromUCSC cannot load the refGene table for hg19 by installing the necessary updates.
input: makeTxDbFromUCSC(genome='hg19', tablename='refGene'), error: UCSC table 'refGene' is not supported
output:
The UCSC Genome Browser changed the hg19 track names to match those for hg38, so the refGene table is no longer listed in supportedUCSCtables. The current release of GenomicFeatures does not include this change. The developers have updated the development version of GenomicFeatures; the change will appear in the next Bioconductor release. To use the updated functionality now, install the development version of R and Bioconductor and then install the development version of GenomicFeatures. Once the new release is out, the issue will be resolved.
After the fix, supportedUCSCtables() shows refGene under the NCBI RefSeq track, and makeTxDbFromUCSC("hg19","refGene") successfully downloads the refGene table and creates a TxDb object.

instruction: Explain how to resolve the error when creating a TranscriptDb object with makeTranscriptDbFromBiomart() in GenomicFeatures, given the error message and Ensembl version differences.
output:
The issue can be reproduced by specifying host="ensembl.org" and biomart="ENSEMBL_MART_ENSEMBL" as in the original call. A patch was applied to GenomicFeatures (1.18.5 in the BioC release, 1.19.35 in development) that changes the behaviour of makeTranscriptDbFromBiomart() to drop problematic transcripts and emit a warning instead of failing. In development the function was renamed makeTxDbFromBiomart(). Your current Bioconductor version (2.14) is outdated; upgrading to the current Bioconductor 3.0 resolves the problem. Using the updated version, the following code works:

library(GenomicFeatures)
txdb <- makeTranscriptDbFromBiomart(dataset="dmelanogaster_gene_ensembl")
txdb

The object created reports successful transcript, exon, and CDS counts. The suggested solution is to update to Bioconductor 3.0, which is no longer supported in older releases.

instruction: Remove the first 45 nucleotides from each gene's CDS region in a GRangesList obtained via cdsBy().
output:
In devel (upcoming version 1.21.20) of the GenomicFeatures package, we can do this:

first45 <- pmapFromTranscripts(IRanges(1, 45), grl)
psetdiff(grl, first45)

This maps nt 1-45 into transcript/cds space and subtracts it from the cds regions.

instruction: Troubleshoot the issue when using makeTxDbFromGFF and makeGenomicState with a GRCh38 GTF file in R.
input: GenomicFeatures, derfinder, makeTxDbFromGFF, makeGenomicState, format=gtf, style=chrsStyle
output: Chromosome lengths are missing when creating a TxDb with makeTxDbFromGFF. Set the lengths or use Ensembl style names. Keep only the canonical chromosomes, fetch lengths from UCSC, assign them to the TxDb, or import the GTF as a GRanges, set the lengths and genome, then use makeTxDbFromGRanges. Finally call makeGenomicState with style='Ensembl', currentStyle='Ensembl'.

instruction: Explain why the UCSC table 'refGene' is not supported when using makeTxDbFromUCSC for hg38 and how to resolve it.
input: browseUCSCtrack("hg38", "refGene"), makeTxDbFromUCSC(genome="hg38", tablename="refGene"), Error in .tablename2track(tablename, session) : UCSC table "refGene" is not supported
output: The RefSeq Genes track no longer exists for hg38; it was replaced by a new composite track called NCBI RefSeq, which contains six subtracks. GenomicFeatures 1.27.11 (and later 1.26.4) has been updated to support this new track. The fix will propagate to public repositories in about 24 hours and become available via biocLite(). The browseUCSCtrack() function also required a fix, which has now been restored and appears to work correctly again.

instruction: Determine how to handle a GenomicRanges::union error on R devel during Bioconductor package submission and decide whether to proceed, wait, or use Docker images.
input: R devel 4.2.0, R release 4.1.1, GenomicRanges::union, error '2 arguments passed to .Internal(is.unsorted)', rocker/tidyverse:devel, bioconductor_docker images
output: The Bioconductor devel branch uses the release version of R (R‑4.1.x), so you don’t need to worry about this issue for now. If you like using Docker for testing it might be worth using the Bioconductor Docker images that are built on rocker. You can use the devel tag for Bioconductor 3.14. As an aside, package development questions are best submitted to the devel mailing list.

instruction: Split a TxDB or GRanges object by chromosome and set seqlevels to a specific chromosome such as 'chr1'.
input: Error in h(simpleError(msg, call)) : error in evaluating the argument 'x' in selecting a method for function 'seqlevels': invalid DB file, makeTxDbFromGFF, seqlevels, exonsBy, split
output:
Both GTF and GFF3 files are treated the same by seqlevels(). For example:

gtf_file <- "gencode.v39.basic.annotation.gtf.gz"
gff3_file <- "gencode.v39.basic.annotation.gff3.gz"
url <- paste0("https://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/gencode/Gencode_human/release_39/", gtf_file)
download.file(url, destfile=gtf_file)
url <- paste0("https://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/gencode/Gencode_human/release_39/", gff3_file)
download.file(url, destfile=gff3_file)
library(GenomicFeatures)
txdb1 <- makeTxDbFromGFF(gtf_file)
txdb2 <- makeTxDbFromGFF(gff3_file)
seqlevels(txdb1) # prints all 27 chromosomes
seqlevels(txdb2) # prints all 27 chromosomes

tx1 <- transcripts(txdb1)
tx2 <- transcripts(txdb2)
# Same number of transcripts
length(tx1) # 114090
length(tx2) # 114090
# The transcripts are the same
all(tx1 == tx2) # TRUE

# Set seqlevels to only chr1 for both
seqlevels(txdb1) <- seqlevels(txdb2) <- "chr1"
seqlevels(txdb1) # "chr1"
seqlevels(txdb2) # "chr1"

# Compare exons by transcript
ex_by_tx1 <- exonsBy(txdb1, by="tx", use.names=TRUE)
ex_by_tx2 <- exonsBy(txdb2, by="tx", use.names=TRUE)
# All exons are identical
ok <- mapply(function(x, y) all(x == y), ex_by_tx1, ex_by_tx2)
all(ok) # TRUE

instruction: Write code to perform strand‑aware shifts on all ranges in a GRangesList, shifting each range by one position from the 5' end.
output:
If you do not mind installing plyranges, its shift_upstream function does it in a simple and readable way.
Example on GRanges:

You can endoapply it to a GRangesList object like that:

instruction: Reduce flanking on both sides of a GRanges object to a one-liner.

You are confusing flank, which gives you the flanking range, with resize, which is intended to resize the range. For example: 

library(GenomicRanges)
gr <- GRanges("chr1:10000-20000")

## get the 1000 nt flanking region preceding the GRanges
flank(gr, 1000)

## resize the GRanges to add 1000 nt to the start and end
resize(gr, width(gr) + 2000, "center")

Use findOverlaps to identify self-overlaps, convert the hits to a graph, and find the connected components: 


hits <- findOverlaps(gr)
g <- as(hits, "graph")
comps <- unname(RBGL::connectedComp(g))


For each component extract the scores and select the range with the highest score: 


scoreByComp <- extractList(score(gr), comps)
sel <- which.max(scoreByComp)
unlist(relist(gr, scoreByComp)[sel])


Alternatively, you can use `m <- which.max(scoreByComp)` and then subset with `unlist(relist(gr, scoreByComp)[as(m, "IntegerList")])` or `gr[m + start(PartitioningByEnd(scoreByComp)) - 1L]`.

instruction: Identify the isoform-specific ranges for multiple isoforms given their individual ranges.

g <- GRangesList(
  GeneA.1 = GRanges(seqnames="x", ranges=IRanges(1,10), strand="+"),
  GeneA.2 = GRanges(seqnames="x", ranges=IRanges(c(1,8), c(3,10)), strand="+"),
  GeneA.3 = GRanges(seqnames="x", ranges=IRanges(c(1,7), c(3,15)), strand="+")
)

u <- unlist(g)
d <- disjoin(u, with.revmap=TRUE)
d$source <- lapply(d$revmap, function(i) names(u)[i])
d1 <- d[1==lengths(d$revmap)]
s <- split(d1, unlist(d1$source))

The resulting specific ranges are:
GeneA.1: [4,6]
GeneA.2: none
GeneA.3: [11,15]

instruction: Extract the positions present only in one GRanges object and not in the other, given two overlapping GRanges objects.
output:
To do this, one way is via negative indices, as I elaborate in my answer on my Biostars. 1. Create the GRanges objects: 

require(GenomicRanges)
gr1 <- makeGRangesFromDataFrame(
  data.frame(
    chr = rep('chr1',3),
    start = c(1,100,500),
    end = c(50,200,1000),
    gene = c('ENSG1','ENSG2','ENSG3')
  ),
  keep.extra.columns = TRUE
)
gr2 <- makeGRangesFromDataFrame(
  data.frame(
    chr=rep('chr1',3),
    start=c(100,500,1001),
    end=c(200,1000,1050),
    gene=c("ENSG2","ENSG3","ENSG4")
  ),
  keep.extra.columns=TRUE
)

2. Find the overlapping segments for gr1 / gr2: 

idx_overlaps <- queryHits(findOverlaps(gr1, gr2, type = 'any'))

3. Subtract out the overlapping segments to leave non-overlapping gr1: 

gr1[-idx_overlaps,]

4. Repeat the other way around (gr2 / gr1): 

gr2[-queryHits(findOverlaps(gr2, gr1, type = 'any')),]

instruction: Explain how to make precede() behave correctly for '*' strand ranges and obtain nearest genes for each TFBS peak, considering strand.
output: People have different opinions on what the 'right behavior' of a function should be. The precede() and follow() functions handle strand as they did when they were written, based on user input. It is better to make several calls to the function while imposing strand restrictions as needed, rather than modifying the function itself. The statement that "for the ranges of x on '*' strand, precede() does the same as precede(., ignore.strand = TRUE)" is not correct. As the manual says, a '*' strand range can match to '+', '-' or '*'. In case of a tie, the first range by order is chosen. For example, with a query of '*' strand and a subject with one '+' and one '-' strand, precede() will match the '+' range because it appears first. If the subject order is reversed, the '-' range is chosen because it is now first. If the documentation does not accurately reflect this behavior, please cite the section and the behavior will be clarified. Thanks.

instruction: Find distanceToNearest without overlapping ranges and check for a flag in the docs.
output:
Do not remove any ranges from the subject before calling distanceToNearest() or you will lose hits you want to keep. For example:

library(GenomicRanges)
x <- GRanges(c("chr1:2-4", "chr1:5-8"))
subject <- GRanges("chr1:7-10")

hits <- distanceToNearest(x, subject)
# hits contains queryHits, subjectHits and distance

# If you want to exclude overlapping ranges, first find overlaps:
overlaps <- subjectHits(findOverlaps(x, subject))
# then call distanceToNearest() on the subject without those overlaps:

hits_no_overlap <- distanceToNearest(x, subject[-overlaps])
# hits_no_overlap contains only non‑overlapping hits

# Alternatively, you can remove hits after calling distanceToNearest():
hits <- distanceToNearest(x, subject)
code <- pcompare(x[queryHits(hits)], subject[subjectHits(hits)])
# code contains overlapping codes; remove those with code magnitude > 4:
filtered_hits <- hits[abs(code) > 4]

# See ?IRanges::pcompare for the meaning of the overlapping codes.
# It would be nice to have this as a flag in distanceToNearest().

output: Use the GRanges method for follow() instead of IRanges::follow(). The IRanges method ignores strand because IRanges objects do have have a strand. To ignore strand in GRanges, just use ignore.strand=TRUE. From the ?follow man page: precede: For each range in ‘x’, ‘precede’ returns the index of the interval in ‘subject’ that is directly preceded by the query range. Overlapping ranges are excluded. ‘NA’ is returned when there are no qualifying ranges in ‘subject’. follow: The opposite of ‘precede’, this function returns the index of the range in ‘subject’ that a query range in ‘x’ directly follows. Overlapping ranges are excluded. ‘NA’ is returned when there are no qualifying ranges in ‘subject’. These functions are returning what they should. For your data, x is a GRanges object with one range at 62789271 on chr11. y is a GRanges object with two ranges at 62789197 and 62789292 on chr11. precede(x, y) returns [2] and follow(x, y) returns [1]. Valerie

input: featureCounts, IGV, fastp, STAR, -T 4, -s 1, -a Hs_genome_chr.gtf, -R CORE, -p, --countReadPairs, -g gene_id, -t transcript, --nonOverlap 0, --primary, -o testout.featureCounts, Unassigned_Overlapping_Length
output: When featureCounts is run with nonOverlap=0, it requires that all bases in the read pair overlap the feature. If a read pair has soft‑clipped bases, those bases are not considered overlapping, so the pair is not assigned. Soft‑clipped bases appear in the CIGAR string as "S" and are not visible in IGV unless the mouse pointer is moved over the read. If the read pair has, for example, 3 soft‑clipped bases, it will only be assigned when nonOverlap is set to 3 or higher.

instruction: Explain how to count multi-mapping reads with featureCounts 1.6.4, the effect of --primary and -M options, and why GZIP error 2 occurs with Subread 1.5.0.
input: featureCounts, Subread 1.5.0, Subread 1.6.4, HISAT2, GZIP error 2, BAM files, TCGA BAMs
output: Both v1.5.0 and v1.6.4 are very old; upgrading to v2.0.6 is recommended. There was a change regarding the --primary option between v1.5.0 and v1.6.4, and the manual has conflicting descriptions. Section 6.2.6 states that when multi‑mapping reads are reported with primary and secondary alignments and both -M and --primary are specified, only primary alignments are considered and secondary alignments are ignored. If -M is specified but --primary is not, both primary and secondary alignments are considered. This is the correct behavior for --primary. In v1.6.4 (and later) you must specify -M to allow multimapping reads, even if you only want to count their primary alignments. Regarding the GZIP error 2 with Subread 1.5.0, the cause is unknown, but it may be due to an upgraded zlib version after the hardware change, since v1.6.4 works on the BAM files.

instruction: Determine whether featureCounts can be set to count only reads that fall entirely within features, as in IntersectionStrict mode.
output: No, featureCounts currently does not support this. It counts the number of bases in a read that overlap with a feature; the minOverlap parameter can be used to specify how many overlapping bases are required to assign a read to a feature, but this does not do exactly what you want. We will extend this to check if bases in a read all fall within the feature, and this new feature should be added to featureCounts fairly soon.

instruction: Count mapped reads in equal-size intervals (200 bp) across the genome and find a faster method if featureCounts is too slow.
output: An alternative might be to use windowCounts from the csaw package. You can adjust the width, spacing, and ext parameters to achieve 200 bp windows and a 150 bp extension. This method is likely not faster than featureCounts, especially since it is not parallelized. However, you can run it once, let it take 10–20 minutes depending on your machine, number of BAM files, and number of reads, and then save the resulting count matrix (from featureCounts) or the SummarizedExperiment object (from windowCounts) for future use. You do not need to re‑run it each time you load your dataset. featureCounts will count any features you provide, so fixed intervals are acceptable. Just remember that reads overlapping interval boundaries will not be counted unless you set allowMultiOverlaps=TRUE, which is important for small intervals. This issue does not arise with gene‑based features because genes are generally sparsely distributed.

instruction: Create a RangedSummarizedExperiment from the output of featureCounts to use with csaw, including any necessary fields for windowed mode.
output:
The literal answer to your question is that it's very easy to construct a RangedSummarizedExperiment from the output of featureCounts (at least, if you're calling the latter from Rsubread):

reg.out <- fc.out$annotation
win.coords <- GRanges(reg.out$Chr, IRanges(reg.out$Start, reg.out$End))
rse <- SummarizedExperiment(fc.out$counts, win.coords)

I assume you did counting without meta-feature summarization (as that wouldn't make much sense for windows) so Start and End should just be numeric values rather than comma-separated lists. Now, for the actual answer to your question, some extra fields have to be added to rse to make it behave like the output of windowCounts when you use the downstream functions in csaw . At the very least, you'll need to fill in rse$totals to specify the sequencing depth of each library, and also metadata(rse)$rlen to specify the final extension length for each read (if you performed directional extension, otherwise set it to the read length). I would say that, if you want to perform an analysis in csaw , it's probably easier to stick with the csaw functions unless you know what you're doing. windowCounts mightn't be the fastest, but it does the job.

instruction: Set featureCounts parameters so that splice junction reads are counted with transcript identifiers and junction counts include feature identifiers.
output: featureCounts uses GTF.featureType and GTF.attrType to define meta features. It assigns junctions to a meta feature if the donor or receptor site lies within that meta feature’s span. Junction counting is performed at the meta feature level, so useMetaFeatures is ignored. To count junction reads at the transcript level, set GTF.attrType to transcript. However, because transcripts from the same gene overlap, assigning junctions to transcripts can be ambiguous. It is better to count junctions at the gene level by setting GTF.featureType to exon and GTF.attrType to gene. The default parameter values work well for most GTF annotations.

instruction: Explain why a small percentage of reads are not counted when using featureCounts with --fracOverlap 1, and why removing this option counts all reads.
output: Your command requires each read to 100% overlap with a feature ('--fracOverlap 1') but the reads you provided do not 100% overlap with any feature. These reads have soft-clipped bases ('S' section in CIGAR) or inserted bases ('I' section in CIGAR), making them have the fraction of overlapping bases being less than 1. This is the reason why they were assigned to the "Unassigned_NoFeatures" category.

instruction: Determine if the Chr, Start, End, Strand values correspond to exons and obtain gene-level location information instead of exon-level annotations from featureCounts output.
output: Yes the annotations are for each exon, not for genes. You can work out the transcriptional start and end positions of genes by using the coordinates of exons belonging to the same gene and the strand information. An easier way might be to use the getInBuiltAnnotation function in Rsubread. This function returns a data frame in which each row is an exon and columns are annotation data.

instruction: Explain why the gene counts differ between featureCounts in-built annotation and BioMart results for the same set of Entrez IDs.
output: The featureCounts in-built annotation data come from NCBI RefSeq annotations for a given genome build. The biomaRt package uses data from EBI/Ensembl. NCBI and EBI are two different groups that use different methods to define what is and isn’t a gene, and where that gene comes from (and how many transcripts it has, how many exons, etc.). When you ask EBI to map a set of Entrez Gene IDs (NCBI Gene IDs) to their genomic positions, you are essentially asking EBI to map IDs from a different group to its own IDs as best it can, and then report the corresponding gene locations. It is not surprising that you lose some genes where NCBI says it is a gene but EBI says it is not, or where both groups agree that there is a gene but they call it something different, so EBI will not map the Entrez Gene ID. The approach also ignores any cross-build mappings you might be doing; biomaRt queries the GRCh38 genome, while the featureCounts in-built annotations may be using hg19, so they are not even the same genome build. It also ignores that some genes are found on multiple chromosomes or haplotypes, and for those edge cases what was done for the in-built annotations may not be similar to what biomaRt does. In short, you shouldn’t expect 100% correspondence between NCBI and EBI/Ensembl because of these differences.

output: You cannot count reads to three different types of features at one time with your current GTF annotation file. However you can get around this by changing the names of the three feature types to the same name, for example you may rename all of them to 'CDS_or_5UTR_or_3UTR'. Then you can count reads to all the three types in one command: featureCounts -t CDS_or_5UTR_or_3UTR -g gene_id -a $GTF -o counts.txt $BAM. This might take more effort than simply summing up the counts you got from the three individual counting.
